purpose: Reference table the scoring engine uses to translate an abstract value (e.g. 'Collaboration') into a measurable competency, its observable behavioral indicators, and which evidence-collection methods are appropriate to assess it. The 'evidence_sources' field in each entry references the method ids defined in evidence_validity_weights.json, so the two files work as one linked system.
methodology_note: The core assumption here is that values cannot be measured directly — only behavior can. Most competencies below are taken directly from Spencer & Spencer's generic competency dictionary (1993), the most widely cited job-analysis-based source for translating qualities into observable behavior. Where a commonly-stated organizational value (e.g. Trust, Inclusion) has no discrete equivalent in that dictionary, the entry is marked 'adapted' and built instead from the integrity-testing and DEI-assessment literature. Nothing here is invented without a stated basis.

sources.spencer_spencer_1993: Spencer, L. M., & Spencer, S. M. (1993). Competence at Work: Models for Superior Performance. Wiley.
sources.adapted_general: No discrete entry exists in a standard competency dictionary for this value; the competency and behaviors below are constructed from adjacent, well-established research (e.g. integrity testing, DEI assessment practice) rather than drawn from a single named source.

values.definition: A genuine intention to work cooperatively with others, to be part of a team, rather than working separately or competitively.
behavioral_indicators: Shares information and credit with the team rather than hoarding it
Seeks input from others before deciding
Resolves disagreement constructively rather than avoiding or escalating it
Adjusts own approach to support a shared team goal

values.definition: Consistently doing what one says, and being transparent about risk, error, or uncertainty rather than concealing it.
behavioral_indicators: Follows through on commitments without being chased
Discloses mistakes or risks proactively rather than waiting to be asked
Behaves consistently whether or not a manager is watching
Gives credit accurately rather than overstating own contribution

values.definition: Same underlying construct as Trust; many organizations use this word specifically for the disclosure half of integrity.
behavioral_indicators: Surfaces a problem before being asked
Explains the reasoning behind a decision, not just the decision itself
Shares bad news at the same speed as good news

values.definition: Accurately hearing and understanding the unspoken or partly-expressed thoughts, feelings, and concerns of others.
behavioral_indicators: Listens fully before responding
Names how a decision might land for someone else before making it
Adapts communication style to the audience
Asks about impact on people, not just the outcome, when something goes wrong

values.definition: Recognizing patterns or connections between situations that are not obviously related, and using a creative or conceptual approach to identify key issues.
behavioral_indicators: Proposes a genuinely new approach rather than the default one
Connects ideas from an unrelated area to solve the problem at hand
Iterates on a first answer rather than defending it
Explains the reasoning behind a novel idea, not just the idea itself

values.definition: A preference for taking action, going beyond what is strictly required, and persisting despite obstacles.
behavioral_indicators: Acts on a problem before being asked to
Persists through a setback rather than escalating immediately
Takes responsibility for an outcome even when it could be blamed elsewhere
Anticipates a future problem and starts addressing it early

values.definition: A desire to help or serve others, focused on their actual underlying need rather than the surface request.
behavioral_indicators: Asks what the underlying need is, not just the stated request
Follows up after a resolution to confirm it actually worked
Goes beyond the standard response when the situation calls for it
Balances the customer's need with what is actually feasible

values.competency: Concern for order, quality and accuracy
values.definition: An underlying drive to reduce uncertainty around the quality and accuracy of work, and to maintain clear systems and standards.
behavioral_indicators: Double-checks own work before it goes out
Builds a system or checklist rather than relying on memory
Flags a quality issue even when no one else noticed it
Sets a clear standard upfront rather than discovering one after the fact

values.definition: Ability to adapt to and work effectively in a variety of situations, with different people, and in the face of changing priorities.
behavioral_indicators: Changes approach when new information arrives, without rigidity
Stays effective when a plan changes at short notice
Works through ambiguity rather than waiting for full clarity
Adjusts own style to fit different stakeholders

values.definition: Actively seeking out, hearing, and incorporating perspectives different from one's own, including from people with less power in a given interaction.
behavioral_indicators: Invites a dissenting view before finalizing a decision
Adjusts behavior after learning of a difference in background or perspective
Names an exclusionary moment in the room rather than letting it pass
Credits a quieter contributor's idea explicitly

values.definition: The intention to make one's wishes, viewpoint, or a difficult message known to others in an appropriately direct and firm way.
behavioral_indicators: Raises a disagreement with someone more senior
Delivers an unwelcome update without softening it into ambiguity
Makes a decision under pressure rather than deferring it
States a boundary clearly rather than letting it be assumed

values.definition: An underlying desire to know more about things, people, or issues, including digging below the surface of a stated problem.
behavioral_indicators: Asks a follow-up question that goes beneath the initial answer
Seeks out a source of information beyond the obvious one
Tests an assumption rather than accepting it
Follows up later on something left unresolved

values.definition: A concern for working well, or for competing against a standard of excellence — one's own past performance, an objective measure, or others' performance.
behavioral_indicators: Sets a more demanding target than the one given
Tracks own performance against a clear benchmark
Changes a process specifically to hit a number, not just to look busy
Cites a measurable result rather than an activity when describing past work

custom_value_guidance.trigger: Shown when someone enters a value that doesn't match anything in this library, so it never gets accepted as an unmeasurable label.
prompts: What would someone clearly LOW on this value do in a meeting that would concern you?
What would someone clearly demonstrating this value do that you could point to and say 'that's it'?
If you had to write one line for an interviewer to listen for, what would it be?
